title: Who will lead us now
content:
As Passover approaches, the holiday marking the journey from slavery to freedom, this may be a fitting moment to reexamine the figure of Moses not only as a biblical religious leader, but also as a historical model of leadership. Such a perspective makes it possible to place alongside him another figure, far more modern yet no less formative in the history of the Jewish people: Theodor Benjamin Zeev Herzl.
At first glance, the gap between the two is enormous: Moses operated in the ancient world, Herzl in modern European reality; Moses led a nation of slaves, while Herzl addressed a people scattered among the nations. Yet a deeper look reveals surprising, almost archetypal, similarities in the figure of the Jewish leader at moments of profound crisis. Both acted ××ª×× a deep sense of mission to rescue the Jewish people from existential distress. Moses confronted physical bondage in Egypt and a loss of identity; Herzl recognized the political and social plight of European Jewry and the dangers inherent in antisemitism.
In both cases, the leader does not merely react to reality, but seeks to reshape it. He searches for a solution even when it appears imaginary or impossible in the eyes of his contemporaries. Moreover, neither Moses nor Herzl acted for personal gain. Both gave up personal comfort and a secure life path: Moses left his status in Pharaoh's palace; Herzl abandoned a promising journalistic career and an established social standing in Vienna's high society.
Their actions were grounded in the belief that their purpose extended beyond the boundaries of their own lives, and that they were duty-bound to lead a much broader historical process. Both symbolize a turning point in Jewish history. Moses embodies the passage from slavery to freedom; Herzl embodies the transition from a consciousness of exile to a consciousness of sovereignty.
At such moments, the leader does not merely guide the people, but redefines its identity and vision. Leadership of this kind is not without hardship. Moses faced complaints, rebellions and a lack of trust; Herzl encountered criticism and, at times, deep skepticism from his contemporaries. And yet both continued to lead. They did not break in the face of crisis, but saw it as an inseparable part of the journey. In every crisis, they also found an opening for opportunity, and the hardships themselves forged their path and strengthened their commitment to action. And paradoxically, neither lived to enjoy the fruits of his labor.
Moses did not enter the Promised Land; Herzl did not live to see the establishment of the State of Israel. Their achievements were realized only after their deaths, evidence that historical leadership is measured not only by immediate results, but by the depth of the change it brings about. From a historical perspective, the resemblance between Moses and Herzl is not accidental. It reflects a recurring pattern of Jewish leadership in times of crisis: a leader who walks at the head of the camp, bears responsibility and directs the people toward a new horizon, even when the path ahead is unclear.
Precisely in these days, when reality appears complex and fragile, a question sharper in its historical character than its immediate political relevance comes into focus: Is a leadership figure also taking shape in our own time, one who knows how to combine vision with responsibility, resilience under pressure with the ability to lead long-term change, as Moses and Herzl did in their time?
